  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Open-ended 'what do you think?' on a research excerpt makes evaluating the thesis, gap statement, and contribution claim centrally relevant; the ClarifySQL framing and two-stage reframing need scrutiny.",
    "R1.2": "The argument chain (ambiguity detection → learned policy → benchmark results → generalization claim) must be internally coherent; logical gaps here would be a core finding of any general review.",
    "R1.3": "The paper asserts specific quantitative claims (73.4% accuracy, 6.1-point lift, 12% reduction) tied to a specific benchmark release (AmbigSQL v3); verifying these claims are grounded in the described experiments is central.",
    "R1.4": "A general opinion on a research draft almost always involves assessing whether the contribution delta over prior work is real and well-characterized; the paper's novelty rests heavily on AmbigSQL v3 being the only benchmark with the cited properties.",
    "R1.6": "Writing quality is one plausible load-bearing interpretation of 'what do you think?'—sentence- and paragraph-level clarity could be the primary concern.",
    "R1.7": "The artifact leans heavily on a specific benchmark release (AmbigSQL v3, Chen et al. 2021) with very precise capability claims; if that citation is inaccurate or the v3 release details are fabricated, the entire experimental setup is undermined."
  },
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Are you asking whether the paper's scientific claims and evidence are sound (contribution novelty, benchmark citation accuracy, evidence-claim alignment), rather than whether the writing and presentation quality are good?"
}

rationale.R1.1: Open-ended 'what do you think?' on a research excerpt makes evaluating the thesis, gap statement, and contribution claim centrally relevant; the ClarifySQL framing and two-stage reframing need scrutiny.
rationale.R1.2: The argument chain (ambiguity detection → learned policy → benchmark results → generalization claim) must be internally coherent; logical gaps here would be a core finding of any general review.
rationale.R1.3: The paper asserts specific quantitative claims (73.4% accuracy, 6.1-point lift, 12% reduction) tied to a specific benchmark release (AmbigSQL v3); verifying these claims are grounded in the described experiments is central.
rationale.R1.4: A general opinion on a research draft almost always involves assessing whether the contribution delta over prior work is real and well-characterized; the paper's novelty rests heavily on AmbigSQL v3 being the only benchmark with the cited properties.
rationale.R1.6: Writing quality is one plausible load-bearing interpretation of 'what do you think?'—sentence- and paragraph-level clarity could be the primary concern.
rationale.R1.7: The artifact leans heavily on a specific benchmark release (AmbigSQL v3, Chen et al. 2021) with very precise capability claims; if that citation is inaccurate or the v3 release details are fabricated, the entire experimental setup is undermined.

parsed.clarification_question: Are you asking whether the paper's scientific claims and evidence are sound (contribution novelty, benchmark citation accuracy, evidence-claim alignment), rather than whether the writing and presentation quality are good?
